questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Write the equilibrium constant expression $K_c$ for the reaction $\text{N}_2\text{(g)} + 3\text{H}_2\text{(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3\text{(g)}$.

choices.explanation: Products are in the numerator raised to their stoichiometric powers; reactants in the denominator. $K_c = [\text{NH}_3]^2/([\text{N}_2][\text{H}_2]^3)$.
This is the inverse of $K_c$; products belong in the numerator, reactants in the denominator.
The stoichiometric coefficients must be used as exponents: $[\text{NH}_3]^2$, $[\text{N}_2]^1$, and $[\text{H}_2]^3$.
The equilibrium expression requires all species (reactants in denominator); omitting reactants gives an incorrect expression.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: At equilibrium for $\text{H}_2\text{(g)} + \text{I}_2\text{(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{HI(g)}$, the concentrations are $[\text{H}_2] = 0.20$ M, $[\text{I}_2] = 0.20$ M, $[\text{HI}] = 1.60$ M. What is $K_c$?

choices.explanation: 8.0 would be the ratio $[\text{HI}]/[\text{H}_2] = 1.60/0.20$; the correct expression squares [HI] and multiplies the denominator concentrations.
0.016 would be $1/K_c$; inverting numerator and denominator gives 1/64.
32 would result from using $[\text{HI}]^2/([\text{H}_2]^2[\text{I}_2])$ or a similar incorrect expression; the balanced equation gives $K_c = [\text{HI}]^2/([\text{H}_2][\text{I}_2]) = 64$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For the equilibrium $\text{PCl}_3\text{(g)} + \text{Cl}_2\text{(g)} \rightleftharpoons \text{PCl}_5\text{(g)}$, what happens when $\text{Cl}_2$ gas is added at constant temperature and volume?

choices.explanation: Adding a reactant ($\text{Cl}_2$) increases $Q$ above $K$... wait, it actually makes $Q < K$ because $[\text{Cl}_2]$ increase makes denominator larger. The system shifts right to consume the added $\text{Cl}_2$ and restore equilibrium.
The equilibrium shifts left when product is added, not reactant; adding $\text{Cl}_2$ (a reactant) shifts the equilibrium right.

choices.text: The equilibrium position is unchanged; only the rate increases
choices.explanation: Adding a reactant changes the reaction quotient $Q$; since $Q < K$ after addition, the system shifts right to re-establish equilibrium.
$K_c$ changes only with temperature; adding $\text{Cl}_2$ at constant temperature does not change $K_c$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For the equilibrium $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4\text{(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2\text{(g)}$ ($\Delta H > 0$), what happens to $K_c$ when temperature increases?

choices.text: $K_c$ increases because the endothermic reaction is favored at higher temperature
choices.explanation: For an endothermic reaction, heat is a 'reactant'; increasing temperature is like adding reactant, shifting equilibrium right and increasing $K_c$.

choices.text: $K_c$ decreases because higher temperature destroys the product
choices.explanation: Higher temperature favors endothermic reactions; $K_c$ increases as the equilibrium shifts right (more $\text{NO}_2$ forms).

choices.text: $K_c$ remains the same; only the rate changes
choices.explanation: $K_c$ is temperature-dependent; it changes when temperature changes. Rate also changes, but $K_c$ itself changes for endothermic/exothermic reactions.

choices.text: $K_c$ increases because pressure increases at higher temperature
choices.explanation: While pressure may increase with temperature at constant volume, $K_c$ changes due to the thermodynamic temperature dependence, not pressure.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For the reaction $2\text{SO}_2\text{(g)} + \text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3\text{(g)}$, $Q = 5.0 \times 10^2$ and $K_c = 2.0 \times 10^2$. Which direction will the reaction proceed?

choices.explanation: $Q > K_c$ means there are too many products relative to equilibrium; the reaction proceeds in reverse to reduce products and form more reactants.
$Q = 5.0 \times 10^2 > K_c = 2.0 \times 10^2$; the system has too much product; the reaction shifts left (toward reactants) to decrease $Q$ toward $K_c$.

choices.text: The system is at equilibrium; no net change occurs
choices.explanation: Equilibrium requires $Q = K_c$; here $Q \neq K_c$, so the reaction is not at equilibrium and will proceed.

choices.text: Cannot be determined without knowing concentrations
choices.explanation: The comparison of $Q$ and $K_c$ is sufficient to determine direction without knowing individual concentrations; $Q > K$ means reverse.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For the equilibrium $2\text{SO}_2\text{(g)} + \text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3\text{(g)}$, $K_c = 2.0 \times 10^{10}$ at 500 K. What does this large $K_c$ value tell us about the equilibrium?

choices.explanation: $K_c$ tells us about equilibrium position, not reaction rate; a large $K$ can be associated with either fast or slow reactions.

choices.text: The equilibrium strongly favors the products ($\text{SO}_3$)
choices.explanation: $K_c = 2.0 \times 10^{10} \gg 1$ means $[\text{SO}_3]^2 \gg [\text{SO}_2]^2[\text{O}_2]$ at equilibrium; products are overwhelmingly favored.

choices.text: The equilibrium strongly favors the reactants
choices.explanation: Equilibrium favoring reactants requires $K_c \ll 1$; $K_c = 2.0 \times 10^{10}$ is enormous, indicating product-favored.

choices.text: Equal amounts of reactants and products are present at equilibrium
choices.explanation: Equal amounts would require $K_c \approx 1$; $K_c = 2.0 \times 10^{10}$ means far more products than reactants at equilibrium.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For $\text{H}_2\text{(g)} + \text{I}_2\text{(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{HI(g)}$, $K_c = 64$ at 430°C. Initially, $[\text{H}_2] = [\text{I}_2] = 0.100$ M and $[\text{HI}] = 0$ M. What is $[\text{HI}]$ at equilibrium?

choices.explanation: 0.080 M is $x$, not $[\text{HI}]$; the HI concentration is $2x = 2(0.080) = 0.160$ M.
0.200 M would require $x = 0.100$ M, consuming all reactants; the actual equilibrium leaves some unreacted H₂ and I₂.
0.064 M is not obtained by the ICE calculation; solving the equilibrium expression gives $[\text{HI}] = 0.160$ M.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For the reaction $\text{CaCO}_3\text{(s)} \rightleftharpoons \text{CaO(s)} + \text{CO}_2\text{(g)}$, which is the correct equilibrium expression?

choices.explanation: Pure solids have constant activity equal to 1 and are excluded from equilibrium expressions; only the gaseous $\text{CO}_2$ concentration appears in $K_c$.
Pure solids are excluded from equilibrium expressions; neither $\text{CaCO}_3$ nor $\text{CaO}$ should appear.
$\text{CaO}$ is a pure solid and is excluded from the equilibrium expression; only $[\text{CO}_2]$ appears.
$\text{CaCO}_3$ is a pure solid with activity = 1 and must be excluded from the equilibrium expression.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: The $K_{sp}$ of $\text{AgCl}$ is $1.8 \times 10^{-10}$ at 25°C. What is the molar solubility of $\text{AgCl}$ in pure water?

choices.explanation: $K_{sp}$ is the product of ion concentrations squared (for a 1:1 salt); $s = \sqrt{K_{sp}} = \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-10}}$, not $K_{sp}$ itself.
$\text{AgCl(s)} \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag}^+\text{(aq)} + \text{Cl}^-\text{(aq)}$; $K_{sp} = s^2$; $s = \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.34 \times 10^{-5}$ M.
$9.0 \times 10^{-11}$ is $K_{sp}/2$; molar solubility requires taking the square root: $s = \sqrt{K_{sp}}$.
$3.6 \times 10^{-20} = K_{sp}^2$; molar solubility requires taking the square root of $K_{sp}$, not squaring it.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: The $K_{sp}$ of $\text{Ag}_2\text{CO}_3$ is $8.46 \times 10^{-12}$. What is the molar solubility $s$?

choices.explanation: This value doesn't account for the stoichiometry of 2 Ag⁺ per formula unit; $K_{sp} = 4s^3$ gives $s = 1.29 \times 10^{-4}$ M.
This value results from incorrectly treating $\text{Ag}_2\text{CO}_3$ as a 1:1 salt; the correct setup gives $K_{sp} = 4s^3$.
$2.58 \times 10^{-4} = 2s$, which is $[\text{Ag}^+]$; molar solubility $s$ = $1.29 \times 10^{-4}$ M (half of $[\text{Ag}^+]$).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Increasing total pressure by decreasing volume for the equilibrium $3\text{H}_2\text{(g)} + \text{N}_2\text{(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3\text{(g)}$ causes the equilibrium to shift:

choices.text: Right, toward fewer moles of gas
choices.explanation: Reactants: 3 + 1 = 4 mol gas; products: 2 mol gas. Increasing pressure favors fewer gas moles (Le Chatelier's principle), so the equilibrium shifts right toward $\text{NH}_3$.

choices.text: Left, toward more moles of gas
choices.explanation: Decreasing pressure would favor more gas moles; increasing pressure favors fewer gas moles (toward products).

choices.text: No shift; the equilibrium is unaffected
choices.explanation: Pressure changes affect gas equilibria with unequal moles of gas on each side; 4 mol → 2 mol gas means pressure changes do shift this equilibrium.

choices.text: Right, because the rate constant increases
choices.explanation: Pressure does not change the rate constant; the equilibrium shifts due to Le Chatelier's principle, not a change in $k$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Given: Reaction 1: $\text{A} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{B}$ with $K_1 = 4.0$; Reaction 2: $2\text{B} \rightleftharpoons \text{C}$ with $K_2 = 3.0$. What is $K$ for the overall reaction $\text{A} \rightleftharpoons \text{C}$?

choices.explanation: When reactions are added, equilibrium constants are MULTIPLIED, not added; $K = K_1 \times K_2 = 4.0 \times 3.0 = 12$.
When two reactions are added (A → 2B + 2B → C = A → C), their $K$ values are multiplied: $K = K_1 \times K_2 = 4.0 \times 3.0 = 12$.
$K_1/K_2 = 4.0/3.0 = 1.33$; dividing applies when one reaction is reversed, not when they are summed.
$1/(K_1 \times K_2)$ gives $1/12 = 0.083$, not 0.75; 0.75 doesn't arise from any standard operation on $K_1$ and $K_2$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: The molar solubility of $\text{AgCl}$ in pure water is $1.34 \times 10^{-5}$ M. What is the molar solubility of $\text{AgCl}$ in a solution that already contains $0.100$ M $\text{NaCl}$?

choices.explanation: The presence of $\text{Cl}^-$ from $\text{NaCl}$ suppresses AgCl solubility via the common ion effect; solubility decreases significantly.
$K_{sp} = [\text{Ag}^+][\text{Cl}^-] = (s)(0.100 + s) \approx (s)(0.100) = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$; $s = 1.8 \times 10^{-9}$ M. The common ion effect reduces solubility from $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-9}$ M.
0.100 M is the concentration of $\text{NaCl}$ added, not the solubility of $\text{AgCl}$; the common ion reduces AgCl solubility.
$1.34 \times 10^{-4}$ M is larger than solubility in pure water; the common ion effect decreases solubility, not increases it.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: If $\Delta G^\circ = -17.1$ kJ/mol for a reaction at 298 K, what is $K$? ($R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K))

choices.explanation: $K = 1.0$ when $\Delta G^\circ = 0$; here $\Delta G^\circ = -17.1$ kJ/mol gives $K > 1$.
$K = 0.001$ would require $\Delta G^\circ = +17.1$ kJ/mol (positive); here $\Delta G^\circ < 0$ gives $K > 1$.
$K = e^{\Delta G^\circ/(−RT)}$; $K = e^{6.91} \approx 1000$. 17100 is not the value of $K$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is true about the equilibrium constant $K_c$ for a reaction?

choices.text: $K_c$ increases when the reaction is run at higher concentrations
choices.explanation: $K_c$ depends only on temperature; higher reactant/product concentrations change $Q$ but do not change $K_c$.

choices.text: $K_c$ is the same regardless of how the initial concentrations are set
choices.explanation: $K_c$ is a temperature-dependent constant; for a given reaction at a given temperature, the equilibrium concentrations will always satisfy the same $K_c$ expression regardless of starting concentrations.

choices.text: $K_c$ increases when the volume of the container is decreased
choices.explanation: Decreasing volume shifts equilibrium position but does not change $K_c$; $K_c$ is invariant at constant temperature.

choices.text: $K_c$ for the reverse reaction equals $K_c$ for the forward reaction
choices.explanation: $K_c(\text{reverse}) = 1/K_c(\text{forward})$; the reverse reaction's equilibrium constant is the reciprocal, not the same value.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following stresses does NOT shift the position of a chemical equilibrium?

choices.explanation: Adding a reactant creates $Q < K$, shifting equilibrium right; this does affect the position.
Temperature changes alter $K_c$ and shift equilibrium; temperature is one of the key equilibrium stresses.
A catalyst increases both forward and reverse rates equally and does NOT change the equilibrium position or $K_c$; it only helps the system reach equilibrium faster.

choices.text: Decreasing the volume for a gas-phase reaction with unequal moles of gas
choices.explanation: Decreasing volume increases pressure, shifting equilibrium toward fewer moles of gas; this does change the equilibrium position.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the state of a chemical equilibrium?

choices.text: Forward and reverse reactions have stopped
choices.explanation: Chemical equilibrium is a DYNAMIC state — both reactions continue at equal rates; they do not stop.

choices.text: Concentrations of reactants and products are equal
choices.explanation: Equal concentrations would require $K_c = 1$; equilibrium means concentrations are constant, not necessarily equal.

choices.text: The rates of the forward and reverse reactions are equal, and concentrations remain constant
choices.explanation: Dynamic equilibrium means both reactions proceed at the same rate, maintaining constant (but not necessarily equal) concentrations of all species.

choices.text: Only the forward reaction is occurring
choices.explanation: At equilibrium, both forward and reverse reactions occur simultaneously at equal rates.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For $K_p$ and $K_c$ related by $K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n}$, for the reaction $2\text{SO}_3\text{(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_2\text{(g)} + \text{O}_2\text{(g)}$, what is $\Delta n$?

choices.explanation: $\Delta n$ = moles of gas products − moles of gas reactants = (2 + 1) − 2 = +1, not −1.
Equal moles of gas on both sides gives $\Delta n = 0$; here reactants have 2 mol gas and products have 3 mol gas.
Products: 2 mol $\text{SO}_2$ + 1 mol $\text{O}_2$ = 3 mol gas; Reactants: 2 mol $\text{SO}_3$; $\Delta n = 3 - 2 = +1$.
$\Delta n = 2$ would require products to have 4 mol gas vs. 2 mol reactant gas; the actual difference is 3 − 2 = 1.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For the equilibrium $\text{Fe}^{3+}\text{(aq)} + \text{SCN}^-\text{(aq)} \rightleftharpoons \text{FeSCN}^{2+}\text{(aq)}$ (blood red), what would you observe if solid $\text{KSCN}$ were added?

choices.explanation: Adding $\text{SCN}^-$ pushes the equilibrium right, forming more $\text{FeSCN}^{2+}$ (blood red); the solution becomes darker, not lighter.
Adding $\text{SCN}^-$ (a reactant) creates $Q < K$; the equilibrium shifts right, producing more $\text{FeSCN}^{2+}$, intensifying the blood-red color.

choices.text: No color change; KSCN is a spectator
choices.explanation: $\text{SCN}^-$ is a reactant in this equilibrium; adding it shifts equilibrium right, changing the color.

choices.text: The solution turns colorless because $\text{K}^+$ disrupts the complex
choices.explanation: $\text{K}^+$ is a spectator ion; it doesn't interact with the equilibrium. Adding $\text{SCN}^-$ shifts equilibrium right, producing more red $\text{FeSCN}^{2+}$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes what happens when an inert gas is added to a gas-phase equilibrium at constant volume?

choices.text: The equilibrium shifts toward fewer moles of gas
choices.explanation: Shifting toward fewer gas moles would occur when pressure increases by decreasing volume; adding an inert gas at constant volume does not shift equilibrium.

choices.text: The partial pressures of reactants and products are unchanged, so there is no equilibrium shift
choices.explanation: At constant volume, adding an inert gas increases total pressure but does not change the concentrations (or partial pressures) of the reactants and products; $Q$ remains equal to $K$, so no shift occurs.

choices.text: The equilibrium shifts toward more moles of gas
choices.explanation: Shifting toward more gas moles occurs when pressure decreases; adding inert gas at constant volume leaves reactant/product partial pressures unchanged.
$K_c$ depends only on temperature; adding an inert gas at constant temperature and volume does not change $K_c$.
